edge: Object detection and few-shot learning are key concepts in the context, where few-shot object detection is being discussed.
Object detection is related to novel class models as the problem involves modelling novel object detection categories with few training instances.
Contemporary techniques in object detection can be divided into two groups: fine-tuning based approaches and meta-learning based approaches.
Fine-tuning based approaches adapt the detection model to novel classes through gradient-based optimization.
The role of loss functions and augmentations is highlighted in driving the fine-tuning process for few-shot detection.
The proposed training scheme focuses on boosting few-shot detection by tuning the dynamics of loss functions and augmentations through meta-learning principles.
The proposed approach yields interpretable loss functions as opposed to highly parametric and complex few-shot meta-models.
The experimental results highlight the merits of the proposed scheme, showing significant improvements over strong fine-tuning based few-shot detection baselines.
The strong fine-tuning based few-shot detection baselines are evaluated on benchmark Pascal VOC and MS-COCO datasets.
Object detection is a computer vision problem.
Object detection has greatly benefited from advances in supervised deep learning approaches.
State-of-the-art in object detection relies on large-scale fully-annotated datasets.
In the FSOD problem, the goal is to build detection models for novel classes with few labeled training images.
In the Generalized-FSOD problem, the goal is to build few-shot detection models that perform well on both base and novel classes.
FSOD methods can be categorized into meta-learning and fine-tuning approaches.
FSOD methods can be categorized into meta-learning and fine-tuning approaches.
FSOD methods can be categorized into meta-learning and fine-tuning approaches.
Although meta-learning based methods are predominantly used in the literature in FSOD research, several fine-tuning based works have recently reported competitive results.
Although meta-learning based methods are predominantly used in the literature in FSOD research, several fine-tuning based works have recently reported competitive results.

The main premise of meta-learning approaches is to design and train dedicated meta-models that map given few train samples to novel class detection models, or learn easy-to-adapt models in a MAML fashion.

In contrast, however, fine-tuning based methods tackle the problem as a typical transfer learning problem and apply the general purpose supervised training techniques to adapt a pre-trained model to few-shot classes.
While some of the FSOD meta-learning approaches are attractive for being able to learn dedicated parametric training mechanisms, they also come with two important shortcomings.

Fine-tuning based frameworks offer simple and reliable approaches to building detection models from few samples.

The proposed tuning scheme uses meta-learning principles with reinforcement learning for introducing learned inductive biases for few-shot learning, suggesting a direct relationship between meta-learning and fine-tuning.

The proposed meta-tuning approach consistently provides significant performance improvements over the strong fine-tuning based few-shot detection baselines in both FSOD and G-FSOD settings, implying an improvement relationship between meta-tuning and few-shot detection baselines.

While the experiments focus on loss and augmentation functions, meta-tuning other learning components like the initial model can be an interesting future work direction, suggesting a potential relationship between meta-tuning and learning components.

The dynamic temperature functions and score scaling coefficients learned by the meta-tuning process show results for each Pascal VOC split, indicating that temperature functions and score scaling coefficients are related in the context of meta-tuning.
Temporally attenuated temperature values are preferred consistently, sharpening the predictions towards the end of the fine-tuning process.
The lower plot shows the learned dynamic temperature functions with novel class score scaling.

The learned scaling coefficients, i.e. µα of the learned ρα distribution, are shown as horizontal lines. µα values vary between 1.09 to 1.2, suggesting that the meta-tuning process learns to boost the novel class scores.
The interpretability of these outcomes, we believe, highlights a significant advantage of loss meta-tuning.
In the context of interpretability, we observe that as the fine-tuning process continues on the few-shot training set, the predictions are progressively made sharper.
